votes.reason: The Python code directly translates the Coq specification, checking the exact same conditions and returning the corresponding boolean value.

The Python code directly translates the Coq specification. It checks the same five conditions: apples >= 0, oranges >= 0, n >= 0, n - apples - oranges >= 0, and result == n - apples - oranges. The Coq Prop conjunction (/\) becomes Python boolean 'and', and the equality/inequality checks are identical. For any concrete inputs, both will return/hold the same result.

Python checks the same non-negativity constraints and equality result == n - apples - oranges with unbounded integers; conjunction and arithmetic match Coq Z behavior, so outcomes align for all inputs.
Both enforce nonnegativity of apples, oranges, and n, ensure n - apples - oranges >= 0, and require result equals that difference; Python ints match Coq Z for arithmetic.
